title: Choosing the Right Tool for Text
concept: Use TF-IDF + Naive Bayes for simple classification; use LSTMs/GRUs for sequence dependencies and context tracking.

exercises.prompt: If you want your AI model to keep track of the context and order of words in a long paragraph, which tool should you use?
exercises.explain: TF-IDF focuses on word frequency across documents, while Naive Bayes is a simple probabilistic classifier. Neither has a mechanism to remember long-term context or sequence order like LSTMs or GRUs do.

cards.title: Simple Math vs. Deep Memory
cards.body: Not all text-processing tools are created equal! If you want to sort emails, you don't need a supercomputer brain. But if you want to write a story, simple word counting won't cut it. How do we choose the right AI tool?

cards.title: The Right Tool for the Job
cards.body: For quick text classification (like sorting spam), combining TF-IDF word counts with a Naive Bayes classifier is fast and effective. But if your text relies on word order and long-term context (like translating sentences or writing stories), you need Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) like LSTMs or GRUs, which have memory gates to track sequence patterns.

cards.title: The Mail Sorter vs. The Story Reader
cards.body: Imagine sorting physical mail. If you just check for keywords like 'Bill' or 'Urgent' to throw letters into piles, you are acting like TF-IDF + Naive Bayes. But if you have to read a complex storybook where chapter 5 depends entirely on what happened in chapter 1, you need to remember the plot. That memory-tracking is what LSTMs and GRUs do.

cards.title: Real-world Uses
cards.body:
• TF-IDF + Naive Bayes: Great for filtering customer emails into 'Refund', 'Billing', or 'Tech Support' based on the presence of words like 'money' or 'password'.
• LSTM/GRU: Great for autocomplete keyboards that predict the next word in a sentence based on the words you typed earlier.

cards.title: Is Newer Always Better?
cards.body: People often think that complex neural networks like LSTMs are always the best choice for text. In reality, for simple tasks like spam detection, a basic TF-IDF + Naive Bayes model runs much faster, uses less memory, and often gets the exact same accuracy!

cards.title: Side-by-Side Comparison
cards.body: Choosing the right tool depends on whether you are just counting words or tracking the flow of a conversation.

left.text: Counts word frequencies. Fast, simple, and perfect for sorting documents.

right.text: Remembers sequence context. Great for translation, autocomplete, and text generation.

exercises.prompt: You need to build a quick prototype to classify movie reviews as positive or negative. You only have 5 minutes and limited computer power. Which should you choose?
exercises.explain: TF-IDF + Naive Bayes is excellent for simple categorization based on keyword counts, and it trains almost instantly.

exercises.prompt: LSTMs and GRUs are designed to remember information from earlier parts of a sequence.
exercises.explain: LSTMs and GRUs contain special internal gates that decide what information to keep or throw away, allowing them to remember context over time.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe TF-IDF's limitations.
exercises.sentence: TF-IDF is fast, but it completely ignores word [blank] when analyzing text.
exercises.explain: TF-IDF only counts how often words appear. It does not care about the order of the words, which is why it struggles with complex sentence structure.

exercises.prompt: Which AI translation assistant is designed correctly?

options.text: A system using TF-IDF because translation is just replacing words one-by-one.
A system using LSTMs or GRUs because language translation depends heavily on word order and context.
A system using Naive Bayes because it understands grammar rules automatically.

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by an AI that doesn't understand context or word order. LSTMs and GRUs are sequence models specifically designed to handle the order and flow of language, making them perfect for translating text correctly.

exercises.prompt: You are building an AI that summarizes long books and needs to remember details from early chapters to understand the ending. Which tool is best suited for this task?

options.text: Naive Bayes because it counts keyword frequencies very quickly.
TF-IDF because it highlights rare words across the chapters.
A GRU or LSTM because they can track context and remember past information.
None of these; computers cannot process books.

exercises.explain: When you need to keep track of details that happened earlier in a long text (like a character's name or setting the scene), you need memory. GRUs and LSTMs have internal memory gates designed to remember context over long distances, whereas Naive Bayes treats words as isolated counts.
